statement.p: From an oceanside clifftop {160\ {\rm m}} above sea level, an object was shot into the air with an initial vertical speed of {98\ {\textstyle\frac{\rm\mathstrut m}{\rm\mathstrut s}}}. It fell into the ocean. Its height (above sea level) as time passes can be modeled by the quadratic equation y={-4.9t^{2}+98t+160}. Here t represents the number of seconds since the objects release, and y represents the objects height (above sea level) in meters.

li.p: After , this object reached its maximum height of .
This object flew for before it landed in the ocean.
This object was above sea level {9\ {\rm s}} after its release.
This object was {409.9\ {\rm m}} above sea level twice: once after its release, and again later after its release.

solution.p: The function f given by the rule f(t)={-4.9t^{2}+98t+160} is quadratic. Identify that a=-4.9, b=98, c=160.

li.p: Since a\lt 0, this parabola opens down. Its vertex represents the maximum height the object can go and when it got there.

solution.p: To find the vertex, we first find the parabolas axis of symmetry by the axis formula:
Next, we substitute t= {10} into fs formula, and we have:
The parabolas vertex is at {\left(10,650\right)}. This implies that {10\ {\rm s}} after the release, the object reached its maximum height of {650\ {\rm m}}.

li.p: The parabolas x-intercepts represent the moments when the object was at a height of 0: at sea level. We will solve the equation f(t)=0 to find its x-intercepts. We have:

p.m: \begin{aligned} f(t) \amp = {-4.9t^{2}+98t+160} \\ 0 \amp = {-4.9t^{2}+98t+160} \\ t \amp = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2}-4ac}}{2a} \\ t \amp = \frac{-(98) \pm \sqrt{(98)^{2}-4(-4.9)(160)}}{2(-4.9)} \\ t \amp = \frac{-98 \pm \sqrt{12740}}{-9.8} \\ t \amp \approx \frac{-98 \pm {112.872}}{-9.8} \\ t \amp \approx \frac{-98 + {112.872}}{-9.8} \amp \text{or} \amp \amp t \amp \approx \frac{-98 - {112.872}}{-9.8} \\ t \amp \approx -1.52 \amp \text{or} \amp \amp t \amp \approx 21.52 \end{aligned}

solution.p: This parabola has two x-intercepts: (-1.52,0) and (21.52,0). The point (-1.52,0) represents an imaginary moment before the object was released, as if it had shot up from the ocean to the clifftop before its journey upward. The point (21.52,0) implies the object fell into the ocean after {21.5175\ {\rm s}}.

li.p: To find the objects height 9 seconds after it was released, we substitute t=9 into the formula for f, and we have:

solution.p: This result shows that {9\ {\rm s}} after the object was released, it was {645.1\ {\rm m}} high in the air.

li.p: To find when the object was {409.9\ {\rm m}} high, we solve the equation f(t)={409.9}.

solution.p: We will use the Quadratic Formula to solve this equation. Identify that a=-4.9,b=98,c={-249.9}. We have:

This result shows that the object was {409.9\ {\rm m}} high twice during its flight: once {3\ {\rm s}} after it was released (on its way up), and another time {17\ {\rm s}} after it was released (on its way down).
Its important to understand the role of each part of this question in the graph of f.
